input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. Main hardware components include lower and upper chassis layers and autonomy elements. Operates in the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) domain of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), characterized by resource-constrained electronic control units (ECUs) necessitating computational offloading to cloud infrastructure while maintaining safety-critical operations for remote testing and management.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three services: 1) Data Monitoring via a user interface displaying real-time vehicle state data, projected driving paths, and detected object tracks with data saving and report generation capabilities; 2) Computation Offloading of the ACC system (lead vehicle detection, speed computation based on distance, and actuation commands) from vehicle ECUs to cloud resources; 3) Remote Management enabling operators to enable/disable ACC, set desired speeds, and issue emergency braking commands. Target users are operators for monitoring and management, and developers for troubleshooting using collected data.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation utilizes NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK for embedded AI capabilities, Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as middleware for inter-process communication, Python 3.8 and C++ for application development, and MATLAB/Simulink for modeling and simulation of ACC algorithms. These tools support low-latency communication, data collection, and safety-critical system management.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy for coordination between devices in safety-critical vehicular applications, as mandated by standards but constrained by hardware limitations.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system employs three distinct entities (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity), each deployed on separate hardware components. The User Entity includes two sub-components (3D Visualization and Control Panel) implemented as individual ROS2 packages, with each Functional Entity (FE) mapped to a ROS2 node. This entity-based structure follows ISO 23247 RA but deviates by extending the Physical Twin Entity to handle general automotive functionality such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system architecture follows ISO 23247 RA, with software components mapped to Functional Entities implemented as ROS2 packages. Each entity (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User) is deployed on separate hardware, coordinating via 100 ms clock synchronization. The Physical Twin Entity handles resource-specific and general automotive functions like Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, while the Digital Twin Entity manages data processing and control mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system processes raw LiDAR point clouds using DBSCAN clustering for object detection and applies Kalman filtering for tracking dynamic objects. Data storage aggregates object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database for persistent, structured data management and real-time access.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The lower-level chassis implements steering and speed control via a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller (ESC), supporting precise actuation with defined safety constraints and communication interfaces for remote operation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from physical sensors (LiDAR, vehicle state, motor, steering servo) is collected by Data Collecting FE and processed by Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component. Raw LiDAR point cloud data is converted into object tracks and driving path data. The Data Translation component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions. Communication between software components uses ROS2, while inter-entity communication occurs over Wi-Fi using UDP.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), motor actuation, and steering servo are generated by the Controlling FE and Control Panel User Interface FE. The Data Translation component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for these control signals. Communication between software components uses ROS2, while inter-entity communication occurs over Wi-Fi using UDP.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: ROS2 protocol for intra-SW component communication; Wi-Fi with UDP transport layer for inter-entity communication. Network latency maintained at a maximum of 100 ms (Requirement R7) and clock synchronization required across all entities (Requirement R8).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Each entity (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User) deployed on distinct hardware components; Data Storage implemented using an SQL database.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process for the Digital Twin begins with defining system requirements, followed by architectural design elaboration and implementation. This case study follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture, utilizing functional entities for specific tasks such as data collection and control.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system provides Data Monitoring capabilities allowing users to view, save, and generate reports on real-time operational data. Remote Management features enable users to enable/disable Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), set desired speed, and send emergency braking commands through the User Entity interface, supporting decision-making with actionable insights.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system complies with ISO 23247 standard, particularly Part 2 (Reference Architecture), which structures the Digital Twin into Observable Manufacturing Elements (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Functional Entities (FEs) within these entities may implement OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) for data collection, device control, and information exchange to ensure interoperability.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

The description lists the specific software tools, versions, and their roles found in the Physical Twin section: NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK, ROS2 middleware, Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink. It accurately connects ROS2 to inter-process communication and the tools to the system's low-latency and safety-critical requirements.

block_2_docs_retrieved:

offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

The description accurately synthesizes the overall orchestration of the system, citing the specific architecture standard (ISO 23247), the implementation details (ROS2), the hardware deployment strategy, and the specific responsibilities of the Physical and Digital Twin entities. The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While 'cloud-based fleet coordination' is mentioned in the abstract as a general motivation and 'external' storage is mentioned as a requirement, the text does not provide details on data exchange protocols, interoperability standards, or integration patterns with external enterprise systems or other Digital Twins as defined in the characteristic description. Therefore, the extraction is correct.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Incorrectly states 'Not in Document'. Section IV.A outlines specific fidelity and validity requirements, such as R8 ('Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms') and R6 (validation against 'ISO 15622').

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly specific and accurate, directly reflecting the source text. It correctly identifies the data sources (LiDAR, vehicle state), the processing components (Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, Data Collecting FE), the role of the Data Translation component, and the communication protocols (ROS2, Wi-Fi/UDP).

The extraction accurately captures the control flow from the Controlling FE and Control Panel UI to the physical actuators. It correctly incorporates the function of the Data Translation component and the specific communication protocols (ROS2, Wi-Fi/UDP) mentioned in the implementation section.

The extraction provides a precise summary of the communication infrastructure, correctly citing 'ROS2' for intra-SW communication and 'Wi-Fi' for inter-entity communication (Section IV.C). It accurately includes the specific network requirements: 'network latency of at most 100 ms' (R7) and 'clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms' (R8). The score is reduced from 5 because the extraction specifies 'UDP transport layer', whereas the source text ends abruptly with 'using U', leaving 'UDP' technically unsupported by the provided snippet.

The extraction correctly states that entities are deployed on distinct hardware components ('deployed on a different hardware component' - Section IV.B) and that data storage uses an SQL database ('SQL database' - Table I). The information is specific and accurate to the system described. It receives a 4 because it is concise but could be expanded with more depth, such as the specific hardware details mentioned in the text (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson) or the requirement for external storage (R3).

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task

does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
